items.q: What distinction matters for the free will defence to the problem of evil?
items.correct: Between moral evil (caused by free human choices) and natural evil (earthquakes, disease) — the free will defence directly addresses moral evil but struggles with natural evil, which isn't a product of free choice.
items.misconception: The free will defence solves all forms of evil at once.
items.random_wrong: Natural evil means evil that is good for nature.
items.why: Students apply the free will defence to all evil. The challenge of natural evil is precisely that no one chose it, requiring other theodicies (Augustinian fallen world, Irenaean soul-making) to address it.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: Are the logical and evidential problems of evil the same argument?
items.correct: No — Mackie's logical problem alleges a strict contradiction between God and any evil; Rowe's evidential problem argues the amounts and kinds of apparently pointless evil make God's existence improbable.
items.misconception: The logical and evidential problems of evil are just two names for one argument.
items.random_wrong: The evidential problem of evil claims evil is logically impossible.
items.why: Most philosophers think Plantinga answered the logical version, shifting the debate to the evidential one. Knowing which version a theodicy targets is essential for accurate evaluation.

items.q: Did Irenaeus himself write the 'Irenaean theodicy' studied at A-Level?
items.correct: Not in its modern form — Hick constructed the soul-making theodicy in Evil and the God of Love (1966), drawing on Irenaeus's image-to-likeness distinction; the developed theodicy is Hick's.
items.misconception: Irenaeus wrote a complete book setting out the soul-making theodicy exactly as it is studied today.
items.random_wrong: Irenaeus was a twentieth-century philosopher who debated Mackie.
items.why: Calling it 'Irenaean' signals an ancient inspiration, not authorship: epistemic distance, counterfactual hypotheses and universal salvation are Hick's contributions. Attributing them to a 2nd-century bishop is anachronistic.

items.q: Whose 1955 paper presents the logical problem of evil as an inconsistent triad?
items.why: Correct answer: J.L. Mackie. “William Rowe” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Epicurean paradox primarily survives in which Christian writer?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Lactantius. “Augustine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Mackie's 'Evil and Omnipotence' published?
items.why: The real figure is 1955. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
